name: European Central Bank (ECB)
summary: Holds 2.15%. TTF gas crashed -7.5% to EUR42.95/MWh on April 15 — LARGEST SINGLE-DAY DECLINE OF THE ENTIRE CRISIS. Deal hopes + Asian LNG imports at 6-year low reducing European competition for cargoes + mild weather. If Hormuz reopens, TTF could drop below EUR35 (pre-crisis ~EUR30). European storage still ~30% (5-year seasonal low). ECB meeting in 2 DAYS (April 17) — the TTF crash gives Lagarde cover to hold rates rather than hike. Stagflation trap persists on paper (inflation 2.6% forecast, growth 0.9%) but the energy input is now moving in the right direction for the first time since the war began.

signals.content: ECB board member speech emphasized 'inflation persistence concerns' from imported energy costs, signaling no rate cuts in April. DAX -11.8% monthly, CAC -10.7%, STOXX 600 -9.1%. European TTF gas at 54.52 euros/MWh (2x pre-crisis). ECB faces same stagflation trap as Fed: oil inflation prevents cuts while economy weakens. (Sources: ECB, yfinance, Reuters)

Eurostat March flash: Eurozone inflation 2.5% (from 1.9%), energy +4.9% (from -3.1%). ECB held rates in March but raised inflation forecasts and cut GDP projections. Some analysts now predict rate HIKES at April and June meetings — reversing from the easing cycle. This would compound growth damage in a textbook stagflation trap. The mechanism: oil-driven energy inflation → ECB can't cut → growth slows → but inflation persists → stagflation. Shell warned fuel shortages 'as early as April.' EU gas storage at ~30%. The ECB is now in the same triple bind as the RBI — can't cut (inflation), can't hike aggressively (growth), can't stand still (credibility). (Sources: Eurostat, ECB, Shell, CNBC, Euronews)

ECB at deposit rate 2.0%. June rate hike now at 76% probability per market pricing — a dramatic shift from the easing cycle expected before the crisis. Eurozone inflation jumped to 2.5% (from 1.9%), with the energy component swinging from -3.1% to +4.9% — an 8-percentage-point reversal, the most dramatic since the 2022 Russia-Ukraine shock. This is a textbook stagflation trap: raising rates to fight energy-driven inflation compounds growth damage, but NOT raising rates risks inflation expectations de-anchoring. Rate differential with Fed (ECB 2.0% vs Fed 3.5-3.75%) supports European equities on a relative basis — lower European rates mean relatively cheaper capital. However, if ECB hikes in June while the economy contracts, this support reverses. CONFIRMED. (Sources: ECB, TradingEconomics, market pricing)

ECB rate hike probability surged to 84% (from 76% on April 2). President Lagarde: 'If the shock gives rise to a large, though not-too-persistent, overshoot of our target, some measured adjustment of policy could be warranted.' ECB projects inflation surging to 3.1% in Q2 2026 driven by energy. Growth forecast cut to 0.9%. Eurozone March inflation: 2.5% (from 1.9%), energy component swung -3.1% to +4.9%. EU gas storage at ~30%. Shell warns fuel shortages 'as early as April.' The ECB is entering a stagflation trap — rate hikes into an energy crisis with already-depressed growth. (Sources: CNBC, Euronews, ECB projections, IndexBox)

ECB held rates at 2.15% (March 19). Raised 2026 inflation forecast to 2.6% from 1.9% due to Iran war energy shocks. Lagarde's 'measured adjustment' language signals potential hike at April 30 meeting. Market-implied probability ~84%. Stagflation trap quantified: 2.6% inflation with growth cut to 0.9%. TTF gas EUR 49.98/MWh still 60%+ above pre-crisis. (Sources: ECB statement, Central Banking, CNBC)

ECB holds at 2.15% (March 19 decision in effect). Revised 2026 inflation forecast UP to 2.6% (from prior estimates), growth forecast DOWN to 0.9% (from 1.3%) — both driven by energy costs. Eurozone March inflation jumped to 2.5% with energy component +4.9%. Rate hike speculation emerging for first time since 2023. Next meeting April 17. TTF gas at €49.95/MWh still 60%+ above pre-crisis. ECB in same stagflation trap as Fed — ~70% crisis-driven. Energy costs flowing through to services inflation. (Sources: ECB, Eurostat, Deutsche Welle)

European markets closed for Easter Monday April 6; ECB policy stance unchanged at 2.15%. The Easter holiday timing means European markets don't have to react to the deadline until Wednesday, by which time the outcome will be known. Europe has essentially absented itself from the final 48 hours. ECB's 2.6% inflation forecast for 2026 already prices the war's energy shock; the South Pars #2 strike + Iran's rejection of the Islamabad Accord raise tail risk that the energy shock extends. Macron warned April 6 strikes 'outside international law' — sharpest allied criticism. European framing shifting from 'diplomatic last chance' to 'diplomatic epitaph.'

EVENING UPDATE: Eurozone inflation latest: energy +4.9% component, headline 2.5%. TTF EUR 52.21/MWh feeding through. BASF +2.7% on 30% price hikes validates pass-through. Rate hike speculation first time since 2023. ECB holds 2.15%. European markets closed Easter Monday — no reaction until Wednesday. Macron: April 6 strikes 'outside international law.' Spain closed airspace entirely. (Sources: Eurostat, Euronews, CNBC)

Day 45, Ceasefire Day 5. No new ECB action. TTF gas at ~EUR44.46/MWh (down from peaks post-ceasefire). European storage at ~30% (5-year seasonal low). Islamabad talks failure may push TTF back toward EUR55-60 if ceasefire collapses. ECB's stagflation trap (inflation 2.6% forecast, growth 0.9%) persists — oil returning above $100 worsens the dilemma. No rate move expected before June reassessment. (Source: Tradingeconomics, ECB)

Day 48. TTF GAS CRASHED -7.5% TO EUR42.95/MWh — LARGEST SINGLE-DAY DECLINE OF THE ENTIRE CRISIS [CONFIRMED]. Three drivers converging: (1) deal hopes from Trump's 'next two days' claim, (2) Asian LNG imports at a 6-year low reducing European competition for spot cargoes, (3) mild spring weather suppressing heating demand. If Hormuz reopens and Qatar LNG flows normalize, TTF could drop below EUR35 (pre-crisis ~EUR30). ECB meeting in 2 DAYS (April 17) — the TTF crash is the most favorable energy input since the war began and gives Lagarde cover to HOLD at 2.15% rather than hike. The energy component of Eurozone inflation (+4.9% in March) will mechanically moderate if TTF stays below EUR45 through the April 17 decision. European gas storage at ~30% remains a 5-year seasonal low — the structural vulnerability persists even as the spot price declines. The TTF move activated edges to ECB (inflation input easing), fertilizer-urea (European ammonia production cost decline), natural-gas-lng (global gas price co-movement), and Qatar (LNG revenue sensitivity). (Sources: TradingEconomics, CNBC, Bloomberg)

Day 50. ECB MEETING TODAY — LEANING TOWARD HOLD AT 2.15% [REPORTED via Bloomberg]. Bloomberg Apr 15: officials 'leaning toward keeping rates unchanged, postponing their verdict on whether the fallout of the Iran war warrants a response.' ECB official: 'layer cake of shocks' — 'very opaque, very cloudy.' Markets: 36.2% chance Apr hike, 76% probability June 25bp hike. Banks model three hikes in 2026. TTF fell further to EUR 41.62/MWh (-1.9% 1D, -24% 1M) — the continued decline gives Lagarde cover to hold. ECB March projections: headline inflation 2.6% in 2026, spiking to 3.1% Q2. (Sources: Bloomberg, CNBC, TradingEconomics)

edges.context: Oil spike transmitted to Eurozone inflation — energy component swung 8 pp in one month. ECB cannot cut rates while imported inflation persists. June hike at 76% probability.
ECB, Fed, and RBI all trapped in same stagflation dilemma. Coordinated inaction — none can cut rates. Global liquidity remains tight.

TTF at EUR 47.51 — pulled back on de-escalation but EU storage at 30% (five-year low). Goldman forecasts EUR 55 for April. Gas prices directly feed ECB inflation assessment. Structural refining crisis (heating oil +3.7% vs gasoline -7.3%) deepens independently.

trigger_points.condition: ECB signals emergency rate hike due to energy-driven inflation
trigger_points.mechanism: ECB hiking while economies weaken would signal global central banks choosing inflation-fighting over growth. Equity-negative worldwide.
